source_integrity.principle: Preserve official or source wording exactly in source_statement fields. Generated teacher-facing planning supports must be stored separately.
source_integrity.ai_generation_rule: When generating new chunks, do not paraphrase source statements inside source_statement. Copy the exact wording from the curriculum source, then generate chunk_title, teachable_chunks, learning_intentions, success criteria, evidence suggestions, and prompts as separate fields.

sections.Knowledge: The facts, concepts, principles, and theories to teach.
sections.Practices: The skills, strategies, and applications to teach.

assessment_scale.title: Year 9-10 Materials and Processing Technology

levels.descriptor: With significant support, the student can use materials, ingredients, tools, or processing methods in a controlled task and identify some safety or quality requirements. They are beginning to understand codes of practice, quality assurance, advanced joinery, lifecycle planning, industry standards, or biotechnology optimisation.

The student can carry out some testing, construction, food processing, or biotechnology steps safely and can describe how properties or processing conditions affect an outcome. Their use of evidence, regulatory expectations, quality systems, lifecycle thinking, and ethical considerations is developing but inconsistent.

The student usually refines a materials, food, processing, textile, or biotechnology outcome using testing, feedback, quality checks, and performance evidence. They increasingly manage tools/processes independently and can explain links to safety, sustainability, industry practice, cultural appropriateness, and user needs.

The student consistently meets Year 10 expectations by independently designing, making, refining, and evaluating a reliable fit-for-purpose outcome. They justify choices using performance properties, codes of practice, quality assurance, lifecycle planning, scientific principles, sustainability, cultural responsibility, and ethical impacts.

The student works beyond Year 10 expectations by optimising a complex materials, processing, food, textile, wearable technology, or biotechnology outcome. They use systematic testing, modelling, quality assurance, advanced techniques, and ethical/lifecycle analysis to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

assessment_scale.note: For year-specific descriptors, use assessment_scale_by_year. Curriculum chunks include year-specific success_criteria_by_level.

strands.label: Materials and Processing Technology
strands.focus: Safe and responsible use of materials, food, and biotechnology; tools, techniques, and sustainable production processes.
strands.coverage_rule: Count this strand only when both Knowledge and Practices are explicitly planned/taught and evidence is identified.

curriculum_coverage_rules.unit_planning_rule: Plan one unit around one strand. This file is for Materials and Processing Technology only.
curriculum_coverage_rules.strand_counting_rule: The strand is counted when selected curriculum chunks/statements are planned with LI, SC, evidence, and marking guidance.

capabilities_bank.label: Critical thinking
capabilities_bank.student_friendly: I can explain why I made a design or process choice.
capabilities_bank.teacher_use: Use when students need to compare evidence, justify decisions, or evaluate impact.

capabilities_bank.label: Creative problem solving
capabilities_bank.student_friendly: I can try ideas, test them, and improve them.
capabilities_bank.teacher_use: Use when students ideate, prototype, test, refine, or optimise an outcome.

capabilities_bank.label: Collaboration
capabilities_bank.student_friendly: I can use feedback from others to improve my outcome.
capabilities_bank.teacher_use: Use when stakeholder feedback, peer review, or shared testing supports learning.

capabilities_bank.label: Practical confidence
capabilities_bank.student_friendly: I can use tools, materials, ingredients, or processes safely and with growing independence.
capabilities_bank.teacher_use: Use when students are learning practical tool, material, ingredient, or process skills.

capabilities_bank.label: Ethical and responsible design
capabilities_bank.student_friendly: I can think about how my outcome affects people, culture, privacy, safety, and the environment.
capabilities_bank.teacher_use: Use when learning involves sustainability, lifecycle thinking, data/privacy, cultural appropriateness, or responsible technology.

project_context_bank.label: Redesign a household item inspired by a historical innovation
project_context_bank.description: Analyse past innovations to inspire solutions for current challenges; define needs, set success criteria, and consult stakeholders.

project_context_bank.label: Product design: circular economy
project_context_bank.description: Design a personal product that supports reuse, repair, remanufacture, and recycling.

project_context_bank.label: Flat-pack furniture design
project_context_bank.description: Create flat-pack furniture that can be assembled without tools, focusing on joinery, CAD, efficient material use, packaging, transport, and user experience.

project_context_bank.label: Soft materials: wearable tech hoodie or bag
project_context_bank.description: Design a hoodie or bag with wearable tech, combining textile skills with electronics and coding.

project_context_bank.label: Processing: biodegradable packaging material
project_context_bank.description: Test materials such as starch polymers or recycled fibres; prototype packaging, assess impact, and use data to improve safety and sustainability.

project_context_bank.label: Functional snack for recovery
project_context_bank.description: Develop a snack for recovery or energy, targeting nutritional needs, food science, taste, texture, shelf life, and cultural/environmental packaging values.

project_context_bank.label: Bioactive fermented beverage
project_context_bank.description: Create a fermented drink with health benefits; use fermentation systems and evaluate safety, bioactivity, and ethics.

project_context_bank.label: Responsive smart garment
project_context_bank.description: Design a garment or accessory that reacts to sound, movement, or temperature using microcontrollers, sensors, and textile techniques.

general_prompts: What tools, equipment, ingredients, or materials require explicit safety teaching?
What PPE or hygiene processes are required?
What quality checks will students carry out?
What codes of practice, industry standards, or regulatory expectations apply?
What evidence will show the outcome is safe, suitable, and fit-for-purpose?

learner_support.privacy_note: Do not store named student information in the shared curriculum library. Store named student notes only in a private teacher unit-plan instance.

ai_use_instructions.purpose: Use this file to support a teacher-facing webpage and to help ChatGPT generate unit-plan options from selected curriculum chunks.
when_teacher_selects_chunk: Display source_statement first as the curriculum anchor.
Offer the related teachable_chunks as selectable small learning focuses.
Offer learning_intentions and success_criteria_by_level as dropdown/multi-select options.
when_generating_lesson_sequence: Use selected chunk IDs, learning intentions, success criteria, capabilities, assessment level, and project context.
Break lessons into Knowledge Building, Sense Making, and Applying learning.
Include formative check suggestions and evidence collected for each lesson.
Keep generated wording teacher-editable and concise.
ai_use_instructions.privacy_rule: Do not generate or store named student data in curriculum library files.

curriculum_chunks.source_statement: Safe and purposeful practice relies on identifying and managing risks, using evidence-based thinking to make informed design decisions.
curriculum_chunks.chunk_title: Safe and evidence-based design decisions
curriculum_chunks.teacher_display_summary: risk management, evidence-based thinking, and safe design decisions
teachable_chunks: Identify and manage risks.
Use evidence-based thinking.
Make informed design decisions.

learning_intentions.student_text: I am learning to identify and manage risks in materials and processing tasks.
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to identify and manage risks in materials and processing tasks. Key words: safety, risk, evidence.

Emerging: I can take part in a Year 9 task about risk management, evidence-based thinking, and safe design decisions with significant support.
I can identify one safety, quality, user, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to risk management, evidence-based thinking, and safe design decisions safely.
I can describe how one material, ingredient, tool, condition, feedback point, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, observations, or checks to improve my work related to risk management, evidence-based thinking, and safe design decisions.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, quality, sustainability, user needs, cultural context, or responsible practice.
Proficient: I can independently create, test, refine, and evaluate a fit-for-purpose Year 9 outcome using risk management, evidence-based thinking, and safe design decisions.
I can justify my choices using relevant evidence, performance properties, safety or quality requirements, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or user needs.
Exceeding: I can extend or optimise an outcome or process involving risk management, evidence-based thinking, and safe design decisions.
I can use stronger testing, feedback, modelling, quality checks, and responsible or lifecycle thinking to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

curriculum_chunks.source_statement: Ethical and responsible choices — alongside the use of advanced and smart materials — are essential for creating fit-for-purpose outcomes in fashion and product design.
curriculum_chunks.chunk_title: Responsible choices and smart materials
curriculum_chunks.teacher_display_summary: ethical choices, smart materials, and fit-for-purpose fashion or product design
teachable_chunks: Identify ethical and responsible choices.
Explore advanced and smart materials.
Connect choices to fit-for-purpose fashion or product design outcomes.

curriculum_chunks.source_statement: Selecting appropriate tools, materials, and joining methods is essential for creating functional, durable, and safe outcomes.
curriculum_chunks.chunk_title: Selecting tools, materials, and joining methods
curriculum_chunks.teacher_display_summary: tool, material, and joining method selection for functional, durable, and safe outcomes
teachable_chunks: Select appropriate tools.
Select appropriate materials.
Choose joining methods that support function, durability, and safety.

learning_intentions.student_text: I am learning to select tools, materials, and joining methods for a practical purpose.

Build: I can practise how to explain how ingredient properties affect the final product in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

wording_options.teacher: We are learning to explain how ingredient properties affect the final product.
wording_options.student: I am learning to explain how ingredient properties affect the final product.
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to explain how ingredient properties affect the final product. Key words: ingredients, performance properties, processed outcomes.
Build: I can practise how to explain how ingredient properties affect the final product in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

Emerging: I can take part in a Year 9 task about ingredient performance properties and processed outcomes with significant support.
I can identify one safety, quality, user, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to ingredient performance properties and processed outcomes safely.
I can describe how one material, ingredient, tool, condition, feedback point, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, observations, or checks to improve my work related to ingredient performance properties and processed outcomes.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, quality, sustainability, user needs, cultural context, or responsible practice.
Proficient: I can independently create, test, refine, and evaluate a fit-for-purpose Year 9 outcome using ingredient performance properties and processed outcomes.
I can justify my choices using relevant evidence, performance properties, safety or quality requirements, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or user needs.
Exceeding: I can extend or optimise an outcome or process involving ingredient performance properties and processed outcomes.
I can use stronger testing, feedback, modelling, quality checks, and responsible or lifecycle thinking to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

curriculum_chunks.source_statement: Food outcomes are manipulated, transformed, or combined by ingredient functionality, processing methods, and production systems influenced by composition and conditions.

curriculum_chunks.teacher_display_summary: ingredient functionality, processing methods, composition, conditions, and food outcomes

Observe how composition and conditions affect food outcomes.

Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to sustainable food production, culturally responsible practices, and food systems in New Zealand safely.
I can describe how one material, ingredient, tool, condition, feedback point, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, observations, or checks to improve my work related to sustainable food production, culturally responsible practices, and food systems in New Zealand.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, quality, sustainability, user needs, cultural context, or responsible practice.
Proficient: I can independently create, test, refine, and evaluate a fit-for-purpose Year 9 outcome using sustainable food production, culturally responsible practices, and food systems in New Zealand.
I can justify my choices using relevant evidence, performance properties, safety or quality requirements, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or user needs.
Exceeding: I can extend or optimise an outcome or process involving sustainable food production, culturally responsible practices, and food systems in New Zealand.
I can use stronger testing, feedback, modelling, quality checks, and responsible or lifecycle thinking to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

Connect biotechnology processes to useful products.

curriculum_chunks.source_statement: Biotechnology processes have benefits (e.g. improved food safety) and risks (e.g. contamination, environmental impact) that need careful management.

Identify risks such as contamination or environmental impact.
Explain why risks need careful management.

Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

wording_options.teacher: We are learning to connect safe practice to biotechnology outcomes.
wording_options.student: I am learning to connect safe practice to biotechnology (using living things or biological processes) outcomes.
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to connect safe practice to biotechnology (using living things or biological processes) outcomes. Key words: benefits, risks, contamination.
Build: I can practise how to connect safe practice to biotechnology (using living things or biological processes) outcomes in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

Emerging: I can take part in a Year 9 task about biotechnology benefits, risks, contamination, environmental impact, and careful management with significant support.
I can identify one safety, quality, user, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to biotechnology benefits, risks, contamination, environmental impact, and careful management safely.
I can describe how one material, ingredient, tool, condition, feedback point, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, observations, or checks to improve my work related to biotechnology benefits, risks, contamination, environmental impact, and careful management.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, quality, sustainability, user needs, cultural context, or responsible practice.
Proficient: I can independently create, test, refine, and evaluate a fit-for-purpose Year 9 outcome using biotechnology benefits, risks, contamination, environmental impact, and careful management.
I can justify my choices using relevant evidence, performance properties, safety or quality requirements, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or user needs.
Exceeding: I can extend or optimise an outcome or process involving biotechnology benefits, risks, contamination, environmental impact, and careful management.
I can use stronger testing, feedback, modelling, quality checks, and responsible or lifecycle thinking to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to test whether construction techniques are effective. Key words: basic tools, joining techniques, materials.
Build: I can practise how to test whether construction techniques are effective in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

wording_options.teacher: We are learning to test whether construction techniques are effective.
wording_options.student: I am learning to test whether construction techniques are effective.
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to test whether construction techniques are effective. Key words: basic tools, joining techniques, materials.
Build: I can practise how to test whether construction techniques are effective in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

Emerging: I can take part in a Year 9 task about basic tool choice, joining techniques, materials, design requirements, and construction testing with significant support.
I can identify one safety, quality, user, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to basic tool choice, joining techniques, materials, design requirements, and construction testing safely.
I can describe how one material, ingredient, tool, condition, feedback point, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, observations, or checks to improve my work related to basic tool choice, joining techniques, materials, design requirements, and construction testing.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, quality, sustainability, user needs, cultural context, or responsible practice.
Proficient: I can independently create, test, refine, and evaluate a fit-for-purpose Year 9 outcome using basic tool choice, joining techniques, materials, design requirements, and construction testing.
I can justify my choices using relevant evidence, performance properties, safety or quality requirements, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or user needs.
Exceeding: I can extend or optimise an outcome or process involving basic tool choice, joining techniques, materials, design requirements, and construction testing.
I can use stronger testing, feedback, modelling, quality checks, and responsible or lifecycle thinking to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

Emerging.text: With help, I can complete a guided task about testing materials, tools, techniques, feasibility, quality, and system-wide impacts.
Emerging.teacher_text: I can take part in a controlled task about testing materials, tools, techniques, feasibility, quality, and system-wide impacts with significant support.

Emerging.text: I can name one important requirement, key word, safety point, or quality point for Testing for feasibility, quality, and wider impact.
Emerging.teacher_text: I can identify at least one safety, quality, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.

Developing.text: I can carry out relevant practical steps for Testing for feasibility, quality, and wider impact safely.
Developing.teacher_text: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to testing materials, tools, techniques, feasibility, quality, and system-wide impacts safely.

Developing.text: I can describe one thing that changes the outcome.
Developing.teacher_text: I can describe how a property, process, condition, or decision affects the outcome.

Consolidating.text: I can use evidence to improve my work on Testing for feasibility, quality, and wider impact.
Consolidating.teacher_text: I can use testing, feedback, checks, or evidence to improve my work related to testing materials, tools, techniques, feasibility, quality, and system-wide impacts.

Consolidating.text: I can explain how my decisions connect to safety, quality, sustainability, culture, or user needs.
Consolidating.teacher_text: I can explain links between my decisions and safety, sustainability, user needs, industry practice, cultural appropriateness, or ethical considerations.

Proficient.text: I can independently make, test, improve, and evaluate a fit-for-purpose outcome for Testing for feasibility, quality, and wider impact.
Proficient.teacher_text: I can independently design, make, refine, and evaluate a reliable fit-for-purpose outcome using testing materials, tools, techniques, feasibility, quality, and system-wide impacts.

Proficient.text: I can justify my choices using evidence and correct Technology vocabulary.
Proficient.teacher_text: I can justify my choices using relevant performance evidence, codes of practice, quality assurance, lifecycle thinking, scientific principles, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or ethical impacts.

Exceeding.text: I can improve or extend my outcome for Testing for feasibility, quality, and wider impact by comparing options and making trade-offs.
Exceeding.teacher_text: I can optimise a complex outcome or process involving testing materials, tools, techniques, feasibility, quality, and system-wide impacts.

Exceeding.text: I can use stronger evidence to improve performance, safety, sustainability, or user value.
Exceeding.teacher_text: I can use systematic testing, modelling, quality assurance, advanced techniques, and ethical or lifecycle analysis to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

li_sc_wording_guidance.teacher_wording: Use original LI/SC when teachers want close curriculum or assessment language.
li_sc_wording_guidance.student_wording: Use student_text and success_criteria_student_by_level when displaying LI/SC to students.
li_sc_wording_guidance.jargon_policy: Student wording simplifies the sentence but keeps required Technology vocabulary with short explanations where useful.
li_sc_wording_guidance.stage_policy: Student stages break the criterion into Start, Build, and Show steps so students can see how to progress.
li_sc_wording_guidance.duplicate_stage_policy: When several learning intentions use the same Start/Build/Show stages, display the stage set once and list the learning intentions it applies to.

stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to test materials, tools, and techniques to build practical knowledge. Key words: testing, tools, techniques.
Build: I can practise how to test materials, tools, and techniques to build practical knowledge in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

applies_to_learning_intentions.text: I am learning to test materials, tools, and techniques to build practical knowledge.

stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to use test evidence to evaluate feasibility and quality. Key words: testing, tools, techniques.
Build: I can practise how to use test evidence to evaluate feasibility and quality in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

applies_to_learning_intentions.text: I am learning to use test evidence to evaluate feasibility and quality.

stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to consider wider system-wide impacts when making material or process decisions. Key words: testing, tools, techniques.
Build: I can practise how to consider wider system-wide impacts when making material or process decisions in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

applies_to_learning_intentions.text: I am learning to consider wider system-wide impacts when making material or process decisions.

curriculum_chunks.source_statement: Design engineers make fit-for-purpose choices by understanding material properties, codes of practice, and how context can change how materials perform.

curriculum_chunks.teacher_display_summary: material properties, codes of practice, context, and fit-for-purpose choices
teachable_chunks: Identify relevant material properties.
Connect choices to codes of practice.
Explain how context can change material performance.

learning_intentions.text: We are learning to explain how material properties affect fit-for-purpose choices.

learning_intentions.text: We are learning to explain how context can change how materials perform.
learning_intentions.student_text: I am learning to explain how context can change how materials perform.
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to explain how context can change how materials perform. Key words: material properties, codes of practice, context.

wording_options.student: I am learning to explain how context can change how materials perform.

Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

Emerging: I can take part in a controlled task about material properties, codes of practice, context, and fit-for-purpose choices with significant support.
I can identify at least one safety, quality, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to material properties, codes of practice, context, and fit-for-purpose choices safely.
I can describe how a property, process, condition, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, checks, or evidence to improve my work related to material properties, codes of practice, context, and fit-for-purpose choices.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, sustainability, user needs, industry practice, cultural appropriateness, or ethical considerations.
Proficient: I can independently design, make, refine, and evaluate a reliable fit-for-purpose outcome using material properties, codes of practice, context, and fit-for-purpose choices.

I can justify my choices using relevant performance evidence, codes of practice, quality assurance, lifecycle thinking, scientific principles, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or ethical impacts.
Exceeding: I can optimise a complex outcome or process involving material properties, codes of practice, context, and fit-for-purpose choices.
I can use systematic testing, modelling, quality assurance, advanced techniques, and ethical or lifecycle analysis to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

curriculum_chunks.source_statement: Critical engineering decisions — like material selection and lifecycle planning — impact sustainability, safety, and long-term performance.
curriculum_chunks.chunk_title: Engineering decisions, lifecycle planning, and long-term performance
curriculum_chunks.teacher_display_summary: material selection, lifecycle planning, sustainability, safety, and long-term performance
teachable_chunks: Make critical engineering decisions.
Consider material selection and lifecycle planning.
Explain impacts on sustainability, safety, and long-term performance.

learning_intentions.text: We are learning to make engineering decisions that consider material selection and lifecycle planning.
learning_intentions.student_text: I am learning to make engineering decisions that consider material selection and lifecycle (from sourcing materials to use, care, and disposal) planning (thinking about materials, use, repair, reuse, and disposal).
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to make engineering decisions that consider material selection and lifecycle (from sourcing materials to use, care, and disposal) planning (thinking about materials, use, repair, reuse, and disposal). Key words: material selection, lifecycle planning, sustainability.

Build: I can practise how to make engineering decisions that consider material selection and lifecycle (from sourcing materials to use, care, and disposal) planning (thinking about materials, use, repair, reuse, and disposal) in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

Build: I can practise how to evaluate how choices affect long-term performance in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

Emerging: I can take part in a controlled task about material selection, lifecycle planning, sustainability, safety, and long-term performance with significant support.
I can identify at least one safety, quality, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to material selection, lifecycle planning, sustainability, safety, and long-term performance safely.
I can describe how a property, process, condition, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, checks, or evidence to improve my work related to material selection, lifecycle planning, sustainability, safety, and long-term performance.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, sustainability, user needs, industry practice, cultural appropriateness, or ethical considerations.
Proficient: I can independently design, make, refine, and evaluate a reliable fit-for-purpose outcome using material selection, lifecycle planning, sustainability, safety, and long-term performance.

I can justify my choices using relevant performance evidence, codes of practice, quality assurance, lifecycle thinking, scientific principles, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or ethical impacts.
Exceeding: I can optimise a complex outcome or process involving material selection, lifecycle planning, sustainability, safety, and long-term performance.
I can use systematic testing, modelling, quality assurance, advanced techniques, and ethical or lifecycle analysis to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to identify regulatory (rules or standards that must be followed) standards that apply to a processed outcome. Key words: scientific principles, regulatory standards, consumer standards.
Build: I can practise how to identify regulatory (rules or standards that must be followed) standards that apply to a processed outcome in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

learning_intentions.text: We are learning to consider consumer standards when judging quality and suitability.
learning_intentions.student_text: I am learning to consider consumer standards when judging quality and suitability.
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to consider consumer standards when judging quality and suitability. Key words: scientific principles, regulatory standards, consumer standards.

wording_options.teacher: We are learning to consider consumer standards when judging quality and suitability.
wording_options.student: I am learning to consider consumer standards when judging quality and suitability.
student_stages: Start: I can say what I need to know or do to consider consumer standards when judging quality and suitability. Key words: scientific principles, regulatory standards, consumer standards.
Build: I can practise how to consider consumer standards when judging quality and suitability in my own work.
Show: I can use evidence to explain how well I did this.

Emerging: I can take part in a controlled task about scientific principles, regulatory standards, and consumer standards in processing with significant support.
I can identify at least one safety, quality, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to scientific principles, regulatory standards, and consumer standards in processing safely.
I can describe how a property, process, condition, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, checks, or evidence to improve my work related to scientific principles, regulatory standards, and consumer standards in processing.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, sustainability, user needs, industry practice, cultural appropriateness, or ethical considerations.
Proficient: I can independently design, make, refine, and evaluate a reliable fit-for-purpose outcome using scientific principles, regulatory standards, and consumer standards in processing.

I can justify my choices using relevant performance evidence, codes of practice, quality assurance, lifecycle thinking, scientific principles, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or ethical impacts.
Exceeding: I can optimise a complex outcome or process involving scientific principles, regulatory standards, and consumer standards in processing.
I can use systematic testing, modelling, quality assurance, advanced techniques, and ethical or lifecycle analysis to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

Emerging: I can take part in a controlled task about scientific knowledge, biological process modification, fermentation conditions, yield, and quality optimisation with significant support.
I can identify at least one safety, quality, or responsible-practice requirement linked to the task.
Developing: I can carry out some appropriate steps related to scientific knowledge, biological process modification, fermentation conditions, yield, and quality optimisation safely.
I can describe how a property, process, condition, or decision affects the outcome.
Consolidating: I can use testing, feedback, checks, or evidence to improve my work related to scientific knowledge, biological process modification, fermentation conditions, yield, and quality optimisation.
I can explain links between my decisions and safety, sustainability, user needs, industry practice, cultural appropriateness, or ethical considerations.
Proficient: I can independently design, make, refine, and evaluate a reliable fit-for-purpose outcome using scientific knowledge, biological process modification, fermentation conditions, yield, and quality optimisation.

I can justify my choices using relevant performance evidence, codes of practice, quality assurance, lifecycle thinking, scientific principles, sustainability, cultural responsibility, or ethical impacts.
Exceeding: I can optimise a complex outcome or process involving scientific knowledge, biological process modification, fermentation conditions, yield, and quality optimisation.
I can use systematic testing, modelling, quality assurance, advanced techniques, and ethical or lifecycle analysis to improve performance, safety, sustainability, and user value.

context_examples.use: Ingredient functionality, food innovation, New Zealand food technology.

context_examples.source_statement: Manpo-kei Pedometer — Dr Yoshiro Hatano invented a step-counting device in 1965.

levels.descriptor: The student requires significant support and is beginning to understand foundational ideas for the year level.
The student shows flashes of understanding and is building capability, but learning is inconsistent and still needs support.
The student demonstrates expected skills more often than not and completes tasks with increasing independence.
The student consistently meets curriculum expectations, applies learning confidently across tasks, and is ready for the next year’s learning.
The student works beyond expectations, applying advanced knowledge to complex or unfamiliar situations.
